coredata.dc:title: Carrier engineering of carbon nitride boosts visible-light photocatalytic hydrogen evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Bao, Hong
Wang, Liang
Li, Gao
Zhou, Li
Xu, Yun
Liu, Zheng
Wu, Minghong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nitride, as one of the metal-free photocatalysts, has aroused wide attention due to its low cost, easy preparation, and excellent optical response. However, challenges of the high recombination rate of electron-hole pair hindered their potential applications. Here, boron-doped carbon nitride nanotubes were designed and prepared by a simple hydrothermal and calcination route. Compared with the bulk carbon nitride, the control strategy forms the ordered nanotube structure, which greatly improved their specific surface area, exposed more active sites, and enhanced the graphitization degree. The transient fluorescence lifetime of tubular carbon nitride is twice as long as that of pure carbon nitride. Furthermore, boron doping carbon nitride nanotubes exhibited a 1.5-fold increase in a lifetime over tubular carbon nitride, which acts a synergistic role with nanotube architecture to further increases the carrier concentration and hinder the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole. Under the irradiation of visible light, the amount of hydrogen evolution of the optimum photocatalyst has achieved 22.1 mmol g−1 h−1, which was 64 times that of the bulk carbon nitride and exhibited excellent stability. This work provides a promising strategy for the development of non-metallic doped carbon nitride nanotube photocatalysts for hydrogen evolution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nitride
Nanotube architecture
Boron-doping
Carriers
Photocatalysis
Hydrogen evolution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of the B–CN, B-CNNR and B-CNNT. (b) The TEM image of CNNT, (c) B-CNNT-3, (d) CNNR, (e) B-CNNR. (f) The SEM image of B-CNNT-3. (g) Elemental distribution in the mapping plot for B-CNNT-3. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The XRD pattern of CNNT and B-CNNT-3. (b) The Raman spectra of CNNT and B-CNNT-3. (c) The content of elements in B-CNNT-3. (d) XPS survey spectra of CNNT and B-CNNT-3. (e) Fitted N1s of CNNT and B-CNNT-3. (f) Fitted B1s XPS spectrum of B-CNNT-3. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm curves, (b) The BET surface areas and average pore diameter of as-prepared samples. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the CN and B-CNNT samples with different doping concentrations. (b) Time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy of the CN and B-CNNT samples with different doping amounts. (c) Tauc’s plot of the CN, B–CN, CNNT, and B-CNNT-3. (d) Time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy of the CN, B–CN, CNNT, and B-CNNT-3. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Photocatalytic H2 production histogram of B-CNNT with different B-doping amounts. (b) Photocatalytic H2 generation bar graph of the CN, B–CN, CNNT, and B-CNNT-3. (c) Histogram of photocatalytic H2 production of B-CNNT-3 over time. (d) The stability measurement of B-CNNT-3 sample under visible light illumination. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Transient photocurrent response spectra of B-CNNT-3 and CNNT. (b) EIS Nyquist plots of the B-CNNT-3 and CNNT. (c) Mott-Schottky plots of B-CNNT-3. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic energy diagram of CN, CNNT, B–CN, B-CNNT-3 photocatalyst and proposed a possible mechanism for the photocatalytic H2 evolution.

head.ce:title: Carrier engineering of carbon nitride boosts visible-light photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nanochemistry and Nanobiology, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, 99 Shangda Road, BaoShan District, Shanghai, 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nanochemistry and Nanobiology, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, 99 Shangda Road BaoShan District, Shanghai 200444, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, 50 Nanyang Avenue, Singapore, 639798, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, 50 Nanyang Avenue Singapore 639798, Singapore

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Applied Radiation Institute, Shanghai University, 333 Nanchen Road, BaoShan District, Shanghai, 200444, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Applied Radiation Institute, Shanghai University, 333 Nanchen Road BaoShan District, Shanghai 200444, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nitride, as one of the metal-free photocatalysts, has aroused wide attention due to its low cost, easy preparation, and excellent optical response. However, challenges of the high recombination rate of electron-hole pair hindered their potential applications. Here, boron-doped carbon nitride nanotubes were designed and prepared by a simple hydrothermal and calcination route. Compared with the bulk carbon nitride, the control strategy forms the ordered nanotube structure, which greatly improved their specific surface area, exposed more active sites, and enhanced the graphitization degree. The transient fluorescence lifetime of tubular carbon nitride is twice as long as that of pure carbon nitride. Furthermore, boron doping carbon nitride nanotubes exhibited a 1.5-fold increase in a lifetime over tubular carbon nitride, which acts a synergistic role with nanotube architecture to further increases the carrier concentration and hinder the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole. Under the irradiation of visible light, the amount of hydrogen evolution of the optimum photocatalyst has achieved 22.1 mmol g−1 h−1, which was 64 times that of the bulk carbon nitride and exhibited excellent stability. This work provides a promising strategy for the development of non-metallic doped carbon nitride nanotube photocatalysts for hydrogen evolution.

ce:section.ce:para: Given the ongoing rapid development of human society, energy shortage and environmental pollution have risen worldwide [1–3]. Exploiting green and sustainable energy is possible to address this problem. Hydrogen (H2) without second-pollution is a fascinating candidate for replacing traditional fossil energy [4,5]. Among the various synthesis technologies, photocatalytic water splitting is a competitive technology due to their environmentally friendly and renewable [6,7].

To date, most of the reported photocatalysts are lack visible light response and poor stability in the reaction process [8]. In contrast, carbon nitride photocatalyst has been considerably concerned because of its appropriate band energy, reliable chemical properties, and nontoxicity [5,9,10]. Nonetheless, bulk carbon nitride was confronted with the challenges of inadequate visible-light utilization and severe charge-carrier recombination [11–19]. Thereupon, diverse strategies have been established to promote the separation and migration of photogenerated carriers [19–23] and enhance visible-light absorption, including morphology modifications, doping (such as metallic and non-metallic) [24,25], composite, and the construction of defects. In terms of structural regulation, one-dimension (1D) tubular nanostructures were thrown into the stage of catalytic research because that their internal microchannel architectures can multi-reflection the incident light [26–28]. It reveals the superiority of oriented electron transfer compared to initial carbon nitride. Furthermore, the heteroatom doping strategy can strengthen the concentration of carriers and heightens its lifetime. Thereby, the coupling engineering tactic of microstructure and doping were further strengthened photocatalytic activity [29–31].

To verify the hypothesis, boron (B)-doped [32,33] Carrie carbon nitride nanotubes (B-CNNT) were designed and prepared by a simple hydrothermal and calcination method [34]. Through morphology regulation and B-doping, an ordered 1D tubular structure of carbon nitride can be initially formatted, increasing the specific surface area [24]. Unexpectedly, even though the band structure of B-CNNT was not changed obviously, their carrier concentration increased significantly to enhance the ability to separate electrons and holes, which is certified by transient fluorescence lifetime test [35]. The optimum B-CNNT achieved extremely excellent and stable photocatalytic H2 production performance [36]. Therefore, our findings pave a new channel for the design and exploitation of highly efficient non-metal-based photocatalysts [37,38].

Chemicals. Boric acid (99.5%, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd), Melamine (99%, Adamas), H2PtCl6·6H2O (99.9%, Adamas) and TEOA (99%+, Adamas). All chemicals were used without any further purification.

Synthesis of tubular B-doped carbon nitride samples. 1 g of melamine and 0.2 g boric acid was dissolved in 100 mL of deionized-water and dispersed for 2 h under ultrasonic conditions. The obtained homogeneous solution was transferred to a 150 mL homemade Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor and heated at 180 °C for 15 h. After the reactor cooled down to room temperature, the product was washed and centrifuged with deionized water and ethanol three times. Then the attained white powder was dried in an oven at 60 °C. Finally, the products were annealed at 500 °C in N2 for 4 h with a heating rate of 2.5 °C min−1 from room temperature to obtain the final product B-doped carbon nitride nanotubes. The resulting materials were designated as B-CNNT-3. Compared with the above synthetic steps, either without the hydrothermal processor without the calcination process, the obtained materials were named B-CN and B-CNNR, respectively. For comparison, the synthesis of carbon nitride is the same step without adding B-containing compounds and nominated as CNNT. Meanwhile, the synthesis process only adds melamine without hydrothermal process or no calcination process, which is denoted as CN and CNNR, respectively.

Characterizations. The crystal structures were analyzed via X-ray diffraction with Cu-Kα radiation (XRD: Rigaku D/max-2500, Japan). Raman spectra were recorded with a star Raman spectrometer (DXRxi) under 633 nm excitation wavelength. Samples were embedded in KBr pellets and then Fourier transforms infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was tested on an FT-IR absorption spectrometer (FTS165). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermofisher, ESCALAB250Xi) was used for XPS analysis. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the catalysts in the range of 300–800 nm were obtained by scanning ultraviolet–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi U-3100) and integrating sphere assembly. The photoluminescence spectra and transient fluorescence lifetime of the samples were studied by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-7000) and highly sensitive integrated fluorescence spectrometer-fluoromax-4, respectively. To analyze the specific surface area and pore size parameters of the samples, nitrogen adsorption and desorption experiments were carried out on a nitrogen adsorption device. The morphology and element distribution of the samples were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM: JEM-2100F, Japan) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-6490, Japan) combined with energy spectroscopy (EDS). The performance and stability of photocatalytic hydrogen production were tested by gas chromatography (GC-2060, Shanghai Ruimin Co, Ltd).

Photocatalytic H
2
evolution. The activity of the synthesized photocatalyst was examined by photocatalytic hydrogen production at ambient temperature. Firstly, 100 mg of the photocatalyst was dispersed in 50 mL of solvent (30 mL deionized water, 20 mL TEOA, and 210 μL H2PtCl6·6H2O) and covered with quartz glass in a quartz container with a volume of 100 mL. Secondly, N2 was introduced into the reactor for 30 min to exclude the influence of air on the experimental results. Further, the quartz reactor was irradiated vertically under a 300 W commercial Xe light source (PLS-SXE300, Perfectlight, China) and assisted by a UV cut-off filter (420 nm) to remove UV light from the light source. Finally, the gas composition in the reactor was analyzed by gas chromatography, and a total of seven times were collected to determine the hydrogen production.

Electrochemical measurements. Electrochemical measurements were carried out in a conventional three-electrode system, and the photoelectrochemical performance of the synthesized catalysts was evaluated using a CHI760E electrochemical workstation with Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, platinum wire as the electrode, catalyst coated conductive glass, and platinum carbon electrode as the working electrode, respectively. The catalyst ink was prepared by mixing 5 mg catalyst, 250 mL isopropanol, 700 μL deionized water, and 50 μL Nafion under ultrasonication. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was recorded in 0.1 M TBAPF6 in acetonitrile solution coated with a platinum carbon electrode of uniform catalyst as the working electrode. After that, a uniform suspension ink was dropped onto a conductive glass for the photocurrent time curve (i-t) test. The working electrode was immersed in 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution and measured under the illumination of a 300 W Xe lamp and UV cut-off filter (420 nm). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed by submerging the working electrode in 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution under open-circuit voltage, using a voltage of 5 mV and measured at 0.1 Hz ∼ 100 kHz frequency. For the Mott Schottky (M − S) test, the working electrode was placed in 0.1 M Na2SO4 (pH = 7) and held at −0.6-0.8 V vs. Ag/AgCl of the fixed range for testing.

The synthesis process of these samples was demonstrated in Fig. 1 a, the melamine and boric acid (BA) were used as precursors and dopants, respectively. After ultrasonic mixing of the two reagents, the bulk structured carbon nitride and doped type carbon nitride (Ⅰ) are obtained through direct calcination, named CN and B–CN, respectively. As shown in Figs. S1a, 1b, a large amount of blocky structure of the CN and B–CN can be observed. Owning to the etching effect of BA, the thickness of B–CN seems to be thinner than that of the CN. Moreover, the addition of a hydrothermal step before the calcination process endowed the formation of tubular structures (Ⅲ), named CNNT and B-CNNT-3 (The optimum H2 production performance in all the control samples), respectively. The nanotube structures of B-CNNT-3 had more regular and thinner tube walls than that of CNNT (Fig. 1b and c), while abundant fragments appeared in the CNNT samples. In the hydrothermal process (II), melamine is converted to cyanuric acid, then produces an ordered supramolecular aggregation structure via intermolecular H2 bonding arrangement, resulting in a rod-like crystal structure (Fig. 1d and e), named CNNR [39]. Furthermore, BA adsorbed on its surface in a physisorbed manner during this process. Then B atoms were extruded into the CNNR backbone during thermal polymerization to the conformation of the B-CNNR [40,41]. The morphologies of B-CNNT-3 and CN were further observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Fig. 1f and S1c). It can be clearly seen that CN are a large amount of bulk structure, while B-CNNT-3 is a lot of uniform nanotubes, with the average diameter of tubes and the thickness of tube walls being about 1 and 0.2 μm, respectively. Besides, the distribution of each element is characterized by mapping (Fig. 1g and S1d). Except for C and N elements, it can be distinctly observed that B and slight O originating from BA are also uniformly distributed along the tube. Preliminary results indicate that the morphology regulation of B-CNNT-3 from amorphous to 1D tubular is successful, and B has been successfully doped into nanotubes.

The crystal phases of CNNT and B-CNNT-3 were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD). Two typical diffractions are revealed in Fig. 2 a. The 100 and 002 peaks centered around 12.9° and 27.5° are attributed to the in-plane structural packing of tri-s-triazine (heptazine) units and interlayer stacking of aromatic units of carbon nitride nanotubes, respectively [9,40,42]. Introducing the B atom into carbon nitride, the interlayer packing distance of tri-s-triazine units slightly decreased from 27.5° to 27.4°, indicating the successful opening of the heptazine structure. In opposite, a significantly XRD diffraction peak at 27.3° of the directly calcined B–CN weakened whereas their diffraction peak near the 100 peaks at 13° almost disappeared (Fig. S2). This unusual phenomenon indicated that the violent reaction between BA and melamine can destroy tri-s-triazine and aromatic units of carbon nitride. Raman data indicated that B-CNNT-3 (I
G /I
D = 1.18) is more graphitized than CNNT (I
G /I
D = 1.08) (Fig. 2b) while the B–CN (I
G :I
D = 1.16) also has a higher graphitization than CN (I
G :I
D = 1.09) (Fig. S3), exhibiting that B-doping enhances electron transferability efficiently. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of all samples showed similar surface characteristic vibration bands (Figs. S4 and S5). More specifically, the stretching vibration from N–H/O–H [43], aromatic C–N, and CN heterocycles are located at 3000–3400, 1150–1450, and 1612 cm−1, respectively. Owning to the respiratory vibration of the heptazine ring, the appearance of N–H is centered at 805 cm−1. However, the in-plane vibration of B–N usually appears at 1407 cm−1, overlaps with the C–N stretching band, and thus cannot be distinguished. Further elemental EDS of B-CNNT-3 (Fig. 2c) also illustrated that C, N, B, and O elements are uniformly distributed on the B-CNNT-3 hollow tubes [40].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to verify the surface composition and electronic structure of various elements in CNNT and B-CNNT-3. Compared with the surface elemental composition of CNNT, B-CNNT-3 was gained extra B elements besides C and N elements (Fig. 2d). The presence of the O element was barely detected in the total spectra, probably the O atoms participated in the formation of other compounds during high-temperature calcination. Meanwhile, there is the appearance of an O element peak with binding energy centered at 531.8 eV in the XPS data for the hydrothermally intermediates CNNR and B-CNNR (Fig. S6) owing to the conversion of partial melamine to trimeric cyanic acid. In the C1s spectra (Fig. S7), the binding energies of CN and C–N in the triazine structure appear around 284.7 and 288.1 eV, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2e, NC (398.8 eV), N–H (400.1 eV), and N–C (401.3 eV) groups were detected in the surface of CNNT and B-CNNT-3, respectively. Furthermore, the N–B signal was detected at 398.2 and 192.0 eV in the N1s and B1s spectra of B-CNNT-3, respectively (Fig. 2e and f). The unique N–B signal implied the B introducing into CNNT just only one ordered connection pathway to form B-CNNT-3. Besides the N–B, there are C–B and O–B signals in the XPS data of B–CN (Fig. S8), indicating that the B-doping of B–CN is irregular when producing without hydrothermal process.

The specific surface area and pore structure analysis of these photocatalysts were investigated using the nitrogen adsorption-desorption method. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the results show that all samples belong to the fourth class (BDDT classification), which is characteristic of mesoporous structures, except B–CN which belongs to the fifth class [44]. The average pore size distribution of all samples ranges from 3.4 nm to ∼4.3 nm, which belonged to mesoporous (2–50 nm) in Fig. 3b and Fig. S9. The specific surface area of B-CNNT-3 can reach ∼25.56 m2/g, which is ∼1.8 and 4.6 times larger than that of CNNT and CN, respectively, indicating that the coupling strategy of B-doping and nanotube architecture can increase the specific surface area significantly. Meanwhile, the specific surface area of CNNT is ∼2.6 times larger than that of CN (Table S1). It is meaning that structural engineering is the main reason for the increased specific surface area rather than B-doping. The large specific surface area of B-CNNT-3 is beneficial to the adsorption of reactants and endow the photocatalyst with more active sites, thus enhancing the ability of photocatalysis to produce H2.

As shown in Fig. 4 a, the intensity of light absorption by different doping amounts of B-CNNT samples was enhanced with increasing B content. From the time-resolved PL spectra of these samples in Fig. 4b, there is a different discovery that the lifetime of photocatalyst increased firstly from 6.60 to 7.69 ns and then decreased gradually to 4.79 ns with the increase of B-doping amount. The B-CNNT-3 at intermediate doping content has the longest lifetime and largest carrier concentrations among these photocatalysts. To further explore the optical properties of B-CNNT-3, the optical absorbance capability of the optimal B-CNNT-3 and different parallel control samples were further analyzed with UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (Fig. S10). The absorption of visible light at the trailing position of B-CNNT-3 is similar to CNNT and higher than that of CN significantly, indicating that the tube structure of the catalyst can strengthen the absorption of visible light. Besides, transformation by the Kubelka-Munk method gave Tauc plots for the individual samples (Fig. 4c) [45,46]. The band gaps of CN, B–CN, B-CNNT-3, and CNNT were estimated to be 2.68, 2.56, 2.65, and 2.72 eV, respectively. From the results, it can be perceived that the bandgap between the various samples has not changed significantly, which illustrates that the regulation of the bandgap by structural adjustment as well as B-doping has not particularly great influence.

To research the photocatalyst inner electron transfer kinetics more clearly, the prepared photocatalyst was subjected to the photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) test (Fig. S11). The PL intensity of CNNT exhibited obvious attenuation compared to CN and B–CN, which indicated a lower electron-hole recombination rate of CNNT due to the formation of the tubular structure. Furthermore, the PL intensity of B-CNNT-3 is further quenched compared with CNNT, implying that the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole can further weaken after incorporating a certain amount of B element into the nanotubes. Moreover, the transient fluorescence lifetimes of the samples were analyzed using a triexponential fit, and the individual fitting parameters are shown in Table S3. The calculated lifetimes of B–CN, CN, CNNT, and B-CNNT-3 are 3.86, 4.83, 5.07, and 7.69 ns, respectively (Fig. 4d), which is corresponding to the results of the PL spectroscopy test. Therefore, the synergistic effect of structure control and B-doping does not significantly change the bandgap, but can remarkably extend the lifetime and increase the carrier concentration, thus boosting the photocatalytic performance of H2 evolution.

The photocatalytic H2 production capability of B-CNNT with different B-doping amounts was measured by gas chromatography under visible light irradiation with triethanolamine as a sacrificial agent (Fig. 5 a). The average production rate curves of H2 showed a volcano type with increasing B-doping. When the doping amount of B was 2.2 wt%, the amount of H2 evolution of B-CNNT-3 is up to the vertex of 22.1 mmol g−1 h−1 and the apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) reached 7.33%, which has a very excellent hydrogen production capacity (Table S2) and the longest lifetime. To further explore the enhanced HER mechanisms of B-CNNT-3, the photocatalytic H2 production of CN, B–CN, CNNT, and B-CNNT-3 was recorded, as shown in Fig. 5b. The order of photocatalytic H2 production performance is B-CNNT-3>CNNT > CN > B–CN, which coincides with the trend of their lifetime. The H2 production of B-CNNT-3 increases steadily with time and retains the stability in Fig. 5c–d. The hydrogen production rate was about 22.1 mmol g−1 h−1 per cycle, and there was no obvious decreasing trend in the photocatalytic activity, indicating that B-CNNT-3 has a stable and efficient H2 production capacity.

To further investigate the electron transfer inside the photocatalysts, the transient photocurrent measurements on the as-synthesized catalysts were performed. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the B-CNNT-3 has a stronger photocurrent correspondence than that of the CNNT. It’s attributed to the B-doping can improve the separation rate of photoexcited charge carriers [11,47,48]. In addition, the hollow thin-walled tubular structure can greatly reduce the time for electrons to diffuse from the interior to the catalyst surface. To further explore the ability of the catalyst semiconductor to transport charge carriers to the active sites, we subjected the samples to electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests under a three-electrode system. As shown in Fig. 6b, the CNNT has a larger arc radius than that of B-CNNT-3 on the impedance spectrum Nyquist plot, which indicates that the B-doped leads to a great enhancement in the ability of nanotubes to directionally transport charge carriers along with the longitudinal structure [49,50]. The flat band potential of B-CNNT-3 was estimated to be −0.59 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) based on the tangent intersection of the Mott-Schottky (M − S) plot (Fig. 6c), corresponding to −0.39 V (vs. NHE). The positive slope of the fitted curve indicates that this photocatalyst is an n-type semiconductor.

The following photocatalytic mechanism is explained as shown in Scheme 1 . It can be seen from Fig. 4c that the bandgap of the sample has a slight change, indicating that the visible light response capability of B-CNNT-3 has not been significantly improved. Then, through the electrochemical test, the reduction potential of the catalyst is no obvious change (Fig. S12), as can be seen from the results that the reduced ability of the catalyst to hydrogen ions has not varied significantly. Besides, the change in the PL lifetime of the catalyst shows that the carrier concentration has changed greatly. Further, the rapid transition of electrons from VB of photocatalyst into CB bring about the current carrier concentration in catalyst substantially improved, coupled with the large number of active sites provided in B-CNNT-3, enables electron reduction of H+ to generate a larger amount of H2 than that of other control samples. Furthermore, the essence is that the coupling of B-doping and nanotube architecture of B-CNNT-3 can increase the concentration of carriers and reduce the recombination of electrons and holes, thereby facilitating the H2 generation reaction to proceed positively.

In summary, efficient and stable tubular B-doped carbon nitride was successfully synthesized by using the coupling technique of hydrothermal and calcination. The optimum B-CNNT-3 has a uniform tubular structure, endowing large specific surface area, high graphitization, and abundant exposed active sites. Besides, the longest lifetime (7.69 ns) of B-CNNT-3 indicates the presence of a large number of high-rate transfer carriers. Finally, the H2 generation ability of B-CNNT-3 was about 64 times that of CN and exhibited a robust reusable ability under the irradiation of visible light. Inconceivably, although the coupling technique did not appreciably change the catalyst bandgap and reduction potential of HOMO orbital, it significantly increased the carrier concentration to give it excellent stability and uncanny ability to generate H2. Our findings can provide an idea for producing an efficient and sustainable photocatalyst to solve global energy and environmental problems.

L. Wang, Z. Liu, and M. Wu conceived the idea. H. Bao realized material synthesis and wrote the paper provided input with data analysis and discussion. G. Li checked the raw data and ensured their authenticity. L. Wang helped to analyze the data and modify the paper. All authors contributed to the preparation of the manuscript.

Hong Bao: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Liang Wang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Gao Li: Visualization, Investigation. Li Zhou: Visualization, Investigation. Yun Xu: Visualization, Investigation. Zheng Liu: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Minghong Wu: Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The project was funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21901154, 21671129), the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (No. IRT17R71). We thank the Laboratory for Microstructures of Shanghai University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2021.04.018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene-based materials for hydrogen generation from light-driven water splitting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Xie, K. Zhang, B. Guo, Q. Liu, L. Fang, J.R. Gong, Graphene-based materials for hydrogen generation from light-driven water splitting, Adv. Mater. 25 (28) (2013) 3820-3839.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Black Phosphorus/Platinum Heterostructure: a highly efficient photocatalyst for solar-driven chemical reactions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Bai, X. Wang, S. Tang, Y. Kang, J. Wang, Y. Yu, Z.K. Zhou, C. Ma, X. Zhang, J. Jiang, P.K. Chu, X.F. Yu, Black Phosphorus/Platinum Heterostructure: A highly efficient photocatalyst for solar-driven chemical reactions, Adv. Mater. 30 (40) (2018) e1803641.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Plasmonic ternary hybrid photocatalyst based on polymeric g-C3N4 towards visible light hydrogen generation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Che, Q. Liu, B. Lu, J. Zhai, K. Wang, Z. Liu, Plasmonic ternary hybrid photocatalyst based on polymeric g-C3N4 towards visible light hydrogen generation, Sci Rep 10 (1) (2020) 721.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecular Co-catalyst accelerating hole transfer for enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Bi, X. Li, L. Zhang, T. Jin, L. Zhang, Q. Zhang, Y. Luo, C. Wu, Y. Xie, Molecular Co-catalyst accelerating hole transfer for enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution, Nat. Commun. 6 (2015) 8647.
